content:
# Luke Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 93-95 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_007_page_001.jpg, LUK_007_page_002.jpg, LUK_007_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** When he had completed all his words into the hearing of the people, he entered into Capernaum.

**2.** And a certain centurion's slave, who was valued by him, being sick, was about to die.

**3.** And having heard about Jesus, he sent to him elders of the Jews, asking him that having come he might save-through his slave.

**4.** And those having come to Jesus were urging him earnestly, saying, "He is worthy [that] you should grant this to him;

**5.** for he loves our nation, and the synagogue he himself built for us."

**6.** And Jesus was going with them. And already when he was not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying to him, "Lord, do not trouble-yourself, for I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof;

**7.** therefore neither did-I-consider myself worthy to come to you; but say [it] with a word, and let my servant be healed.

**8.** For I also am a man set under authority, having under myself soldiers, and I say to this [one], 'Go,' and he goes, and to another, 'Come,' and he comes, and to my slave, 'Do this,' and he does [it]."

**9.** And having heard these-things, Jesus marveled at him, and having turned to the crowd following him, he said, "I say to you, not even in Israel have I found such great faith."

**10.** And those having been sent, having returned to the house, found the slave being-healthy.

**11.** And it came-to-pass on the next [day], he went into a city being-called Nain, and his disciples were going-with him, and a large crowd.

**12.** And as he drew-near to the gate of the city, and behold, one having died was being carried-out, an only-begotten son of his mother, and she was a widow; and a considerable crowd of the city was with her.

**13.** And having seen her, the Lord had-compassion upon her and said to her, "Do not weep."

**14.** And having come-near, he touched the bier, and those carrying [it] stood-still. And he said, "Young-man, to you I say, be raised."

**15.** And the dead-man sat-up and began to speak. And he gave him to his mother.

**16.** And fear seized all, and they were glorifying God, saying, "A great prophet has been raised among us," and, "God has visited his people."

**17.** And this word went-out in all Judea concerning him, and all the surrounding-region.

**18.** And the disciples of John reported to him concerning all these-things.

**19.** And having summoned two certain [ones] of his disciples, John sent [them] to the Lord, saying, "Are you the coming [one], or are-we-to-expect another?"

**20.** And having come to him, the men said, "John the Baptist sent us to you, saying, 'Are you the coming [one], or are-we-to-expect another?'"

**21.** In that hour he healed many from diseases and afflictions and evil spirits, and to many blind [ones] he granted to see.

**22.** And having answered, he said to them, "Having gone, report to John what you saw and heard: blind [ones] see-again, lame [ones] walk, lepers are cleansed, and deaf [ones] hear, dead [ones] are raised, poor [ones] are evangelized;

**23.** and blessed is whoever is not offended in me."

**24.** And when the messengers of John had departed, he began to say to the crowds concerning John, "What did-you-go-out into the wilderness to behold? A reed being shaken by [the] wind?

**25.** But what did-you-go-out to see? A man in soft garments having-been-clothed? Behold, those in splendid clothing and luxury being [are] in the palaces.

**26.** But what did-you-go-out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and more-than a prophet.

**27.** This is [he] concerning whom it has been written, 'Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way before you.'

**28.** I say to you, among [those] born of women, no-one is greater than John; but the least in the kingdom of God is greater than he."

**29.** And all the people having heard, and the tax-collectors, justified God, having been baptized [with] the baptism of John;

**30.** but the Pharisees and the lawyers rejected the counsel of God for themselves, not having been baptized by him.

**31.** "To what therefore shall-I-liken the men of this generation, and to what are they like?

**32.** They are like children sitting in [the] marketplace and calling-to one-another, who say, 'We piped to you, and you did not dance; we lamented, and you did not weep.'

**33.** For John the Baptist has come neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, 'He has a demon.'

**34.** The Son of Man has come eating and drinking, and you say, 'Behold, a man [who is] a glutton and a wine-drinker, a friend of tax-collectors and sinners.'

**35.** And wisdom was justified from all her children."

**36.** And a certain [one] of the Pharisees was asking him that he might eat with him. And having entered into the house of the Pharisee, he reclined.

**37.** And behold, a woman who was in the city, a sinner, and having known that he reclines in the house of the Pharisee, having brought an alabaster-flask of ointment,

**38.** and having stood behind at his feet, weeping, she began to wet his feet with the tears, and with the hair of her head she was wiping [them], and was kissing his feet, and was anointing [them] with the ointment.

**39.** And having seen [this], the Pharisee, the [one] having invited him, said within himself, saying, "This [man], if he were a prophet, would-have-known who and what-sort [of woman] [this one is] who touches him, that she is a sinner."

**40.** And having answered, Jesus said to him, "Simon, I have something to say to you." And he says, "Teacher, speak."

**41.** "Two debtors were [indebted] to a certain money-lender. The one owed five-hundred denarii, and the other fifty.

**42.** [When] they were not having [means] to repay, he graciously-forgave both. Which of them therefore will love him more?"

**43.** Simon having answered said, "I suppose the [one] to whom he graciously-forgave the more." And he said to him, "You have judged rightly."

**44.** And having turned to the woman, he said to Simon, "Do-you-see this woman? I entered into your house; water for my feet you did not give. But this [woman] with [her] tears wet my feet, and with her hair she wiped [them].

**45.** A kiss you did not give to me; but this [woman], from [the time] I entered, has not ceased kissing my feet.

**46.** With oil you did not anoint my head; but this [woman] with ointment anointed my feet.

**47.** For-this-reason I say to you, her sins, which [are] many, have been forgiven, because she loved much; but to whom little is forgiven, [he] loves little."

**48.** And he said to her, "Your sins have been forgiven."

**49.** And those reclining-together began to say within themselves, "Who is this who even forgives sins?"

**50.** And he said to the woman, "Your faith has saved you; go in peace."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **7:1 — "When he had completed all his words"**: Sinaiticus reads επειδη επληρωσεν παντα τα ρηματα αυτου — "when he had fulfilled/completed all his words." The verb πληροω ("to fill, complete, fulfill") is stronger than a simple "finished," carrying the sense that the teaching was brought to its fullness.

2. **7:2 — "valued by him" (εντιμος αυτω)**: Sinaiticus reads εντιμος, meaning "valued, honored, precious." This is not merely a servant but one held in special esteem by the centurion — a detail Luke alone provides to establish the emotional weight of the request.

3. **7:3 — "save-through" (διασωση)**: Sinaiticus reads διασωση, the aorist subjunctive of διασωζω ("to save through, to bring safely through"). This compound verb is stronger than simple σωζω; it implies deliverance through the crisis of illness. Some manuscripts read σωση ("save/heal"), but Sinaiticus preserves the intensified form.

4. **7:4 — "He is worthy"**: Sinaiticus reads αξιος εστιν ω παρεξη τουτο — "he is worthy [that] you should grant this to him." The Jewish elders advocate for a Gentile centurion as "worthy" (αξιος), which contrasts sharply with the centurion's own self-assessment in v. 6: "I am not worthy" (ουκ ειμι ικανος).

5. **7:5 — "the synagogue he himself built"**: Sinaiticus reads την συναγωγην αυτος ωκοδομησεν ημιν — "the synagogue he himself built for us." The emphatic αυτος ("he himself") stresses the centurion's personal investment. Archaeological evidence confirms that Gentile benefactors sometimes funded synagogue construction.

6. **7:6 — "do not trouble-yourself" (μη σκυλλου)**: Sinaiticus reads μη σκυλλου, present middle imperative — "stop troubling yourself" or "do not go on being troubled." The verb σκυλλω literally means "to flay, to skin" and by extension "to weary, to harass."

7. **7:7 — "say [it] with a word" (ειπε λογω)**: Sinaiticus reads αλλα ειπε λογω. The centurion understands authority as operating through the spoken command. The dative λογω ("by a word") parallels military orders — a single authoritative word suffices.

8. **7:9 — "not even in Israel"**: Sinaiticus reads ουδε εν τω Ισραηλ τοσαυτην πιστιν ευρον — "not even in Israel have I found such great faith." This is a remarkable declaration: a Gentile soldier surpasses all Israel in faith. Sinaiticus lacks the addition "I say to you" found in some later manuscripts before the statement.

9. **7:10 — "being-healthy" (υγιαινοντα)**: Sinaiticus reads υγιαινοντα, the present active participle of υγιαινω — "being healthy/sound." The present tense implies the slave was found in an ongoing state of health, not merely "recovered" but fully well.

10. **7:11 — "on the next [day]" (εν τω εξης)**: Sinaiticus reads εν τω εξης — "on the next [day]," with ημερα ("day") implied. Some manuscripts read τη εξης ημερα explicitly. Sinaiticus also reads εν τη followed by the sequence, placing this event in direct temporal succession to the centurion's healing.

11. **7:11 — "Nain"**: Sinaiticus reads Ναιν. This city is mentioned only here in the entire New Testament. It is identified with modern Nein, a village southeast of Nazareth near the foot of the Hill of Moreh — near where Elisha raised the Shunammite woman's son (2 Kings 4:8-37), creating an intentional prophetic parallel.

12. **7:12 — "only-begotten" (μονογενης)**: Sinaiticus reads μονογενης, the same word used of Jesus in John 3:16. The term means "one of a kind, only-born" — this was her sole son, and she a widow, making her loss total: no husband, no son, no provider.

13. **7:13 — "the Lord" (ο κυριος)**: Sinaiticus reads ο κυριος — "the Lord." This is significant: Luke uses the full title "the Lord" (not merely "Jesus") in the narrative voice here, at the moment of supreme compassion. The title carries theological weight in context.

14. **7:14 — "be raised" (εγερθητι)**: Sinaiticus reads εγερθητι, the aorist passive imperative of εγειρω — literally "be raised." The passive voice implies divine action: not "rise by your own power" but "be raised [by God]." This is the same verb-form used for resurrection.

15. **7:15 — "the dead-man sat-up" (ανεκαθισεν ο νεκρος)**: Sinaiticus reads ανεκαθισεν ο νεκρος — "the dead-man sat up." The verb ανακαθιζω appears only here and in Acts 9:40 in the New Testament. Luke the physician uses precise physical language: the corpse sat upright on the bier.

16. **7:16 — "A great prophet has been raised among us"**: Sinaiticus reads προφητης μεγας ηγερθη εν ημιν. The crowd uses ηγερθη ("has been raised"), the same root verb as Jesus' command in v. 14, creating a double meaning: a prophet has "arisen" even as he has "raised" the dead. The crowd echoes the Elijah-Elisha narratives (1 Kings 17:17-24; 2 Kings 4:32-37).

17. **7:16 — "God has visited his people"**: Sinaiticus reads επεσκεψατο ο θεος τον λαον αυτου — "God visited his people." The verb επισκεπτομαι ("to visit, to look upon with care") echoes Zechariah's prophecy in Luke 1:68 (επεσκεψατο) and 1:78 (επισκεψεται). Luke frames this as fulfillment.

18. **7:19 — "the coming [one]" (ο ερχομενος)**: Sinaiticus reads ο ερχομενος — the present participle with the article, functioning as a messianic title: "the Coming One." This draws on Psalm 118:26, Malachi 3:1, and Habakkuk 2:3. John's question from prison is not doubt but a request for clarification of messianic identity.

19. **7:19 — "to the Lord" (προς τον κυριον)**: Sinaiticus reads προς τον κυριον — John sends his disciples "to the Lord." Again Luke uses the full christological title in narrative, where Matthew 11:2 reads "to the Christ." These are distinct theological framings.

20. **7:21 — "granted to see" (εχαρισατο βλεπειν)**: Sinaiticus reads εχαρισατο βλεπειν — "he graciously-granted [them] to see." The verb χαριζομαι means "to grant as a favor, to give graciously." Sight is presented not merely as healing but as a gift of grace.

21. **7:22 — "poor [ones] are evangelized" (πτωχοι ευαγγελιζονται)**: Sinaiticus reads πτωχοι ευαγγελιζονται — the poor "are being evangelized/are having good news proclaimed to them." This echoes Isaiah 61:1 (LXX) and Luke 4:18. The passive voice indicates the poor are recipients of divine action.

22. **7:27 — Malachi citation**: Sinaiticus reads the quotation from Malachi 3:1 with κατασκευασει ("will prepare/construct"), emphasizing active construction of the way. The pronoun shifts — "my messenger" (God speaking) and "your way" (addressed to the Messiah) — reveal a trinitarian dynamic embedded in the prophetic text.

23. **7:28 — "no-one is greater than John"**: Sinaiticus reads μειζων εν γεννητοις γυναικων Ιωαννου ουδεις εστιν — "greater among [those] born of women than John no-one is." Sinaiticus omits "the Baptist" here and also omits "prophet" before "John" found in some manuscripts. The word order places μειζων ("greater") emphatically first.

24. **7:29 — "justified God" (εδικαιωσαν τον θεον)**: Sinaiticus reads εδικαιωσαν τον θεον — "they justified God," meaning they declared God righteous/vindicated God's purposes by submitting to John's baptism. This unusual phrase (humans "justifying" God) appears also in Luke 16:15 in reverse.

25. **7:30 — "the counsel of God" (την βουλην του θεου)**: Sinaiticus reads την βουλην του θεου ηθετησαν εις εαυτους — "they set aside the counsel/purpose of God for/against themselves." The verb αθετεω means "to nullify, to reject as invalid." The Pharisees and lawyers did not merely ignore but actively annulled God's plan — but only "for themselves" (εις εαυτους), not universally.

26. **7:32 — "sitting in [the] marketplace"**: Sinaiticus reads παιδιοις τοις εν αγορα καθημενοις — "children sitting in the marketplace." The image is of children playing wedding and funeral games: piping (wedding music) and lamenting (funeral dirges). Neither John's austerity nor Jesus' fellowship satisfies the critics.

27. **7:33 — "neither eating bread nor drinking wine"**: Sinaiticus reads μητε αρτον εσθιων μητε οινον πινων — present participles indicating John's habitual lifestyle. Luke specifies "bread" and "wine" where Matthew 11:18 has simply "eating" and "drinking," making the asceticism more concrete.

28. **7:34 — "a glutton and a wine-drinker" (φαγος και οινοποτης)**: Sinaiticus reads ανθρωπος φαγος και οινοποτης. The word φαγος ("glutton") and οινοποτης ("wine-drinker") echo the charges against the rebellious son in Deuteronomy 21:20 (LXX), implying the opponents level a capital accusation.

29. **7:35 — "from all her children"**: Sinaiticus reads απο παντων των τεκνων αυτης — "from all her children." Some manuscripts (including B) read "works" (εργων) instead of "children" (τεκνων). Sinaiticus preserves "children," meaning Wisdom is vindicated by those who receive her — both John's followers and Jesus' disciples.

30. **7:36 — "he reclined" (κατεκλιθη)**: Sinaiticus reads κατεκλιθη — "he reclined [at table]." The aorist passive of κατακλινω indicates the formal dining posture: reclining on a couch with feet extending behind and away from the table. This physical arrangement is essential to understanding how the woman could approach his feet (v. 38).

31. **7:37 — "a woman who was in the city, a sinner"**: Sinaiticus reads γυνη ητις ην εν τη πολει αμαρτωλος — "a woman who was in the city, a sinner." The construction places αμαρτωλος ("sinner") in predicate position: she was known in the city as a sinner. Tradition identifies her as a prostitute, though the text does not specify the nature of her sin. She is not identified as Mary Magdalene (introduced separately in 8:2).

32. **7:38 — "she began to wet his feet with the tears"**: Sinaiticus reads τοις δακρυσιν ηρξατο βρεχειν τους ποδας αυτου — "with the tears she began to wet his feet." The article τοις before δακρυσιν ("the tears") may indicate tears already flowing before she reached him, or tears of a specific, known occasion. The verb βρεχω normally means "to rain upon" — her tears fall like rain.

33. **7:39 — "the Pharisee, the [one] having invited him"**: Sinaiticus reads ο Φαρισαιος ο καλεσας αυτον. The Pharisee is here named for the first time as the host, establishing the irony: he invited Jesus yet judges him. His reasoning uses a second-class (contrary-to-fact) conditional: ει ην προφητης — "if he were a prophet" (implying "but he is not").

34. **7:41 — "five-hundred denarii"**: Sinaiticus reads πεντακοσια δηναρια. A denarius was roughly a day's wage for a laborer. Five hundred denarii represents nearly two years' wages; fifty denarii about two months'. The disparity is tenfold, making the parable's point unmistakable.

35. **7:42 — "graciously-forgave" (εχαρισατο)**: Sinaiticus reads εχαρισατο, the same verb used in v. 21 for granting sight. The forgiveness of debt is an act of χαρις — grace. Both debtors are forgiven entirely, not partially; the question is only which will respond with greater love.

36. **7:44 — "water for my feet you did not give"**: Sinaiticus reads υδωρ μοι επι ποδας ουκ εδωκας — "water to me upon [my] feet you did not give." Jesus contrasts the Pharisee's failure in three basic hospitality customs: water for feet (v. 44), a kiss of greeting (v. 45), and oil for the head (v. 46). The woman exceeded all three — with tears, with kisses, and with costly ointment.

37. **7:47 — "because she loved much" (οτι ηγαπησεν πολυ)**: Sinaiticus reads οτι ηγαπησεν πολυ. The causal conjunction οτι is ambiguous: does her love cause the forgiveness, or is her love the evidence of prior forgiveness? The parable of the debtors (vv. 41-43) establishes that forgiveness precedes love. Therefore οτι here means "as-is-evidenced-by-the-fact-that" — her great love demonstrates that she has already been forgiven much.

38. **7:48 — "Your sins have been forgiven" (αφεωνται σου αι αμαρτιαι)**: Sinaiticus reads αφεωνται — the perfect passive indicative of αφιημι: "have been forgiven [and remain forgiven]." The perfect tense is significant: the forgiveness is not new but is being publicly declared. Jesus does not say "I forgive you" but announces a state of forgiveness already accomplished.

39. **7:49 — "Who is this who even forgives sins?"**: Sinaiticus reads τις ουτος εστιν ος και αμαρτιας αφιησιν — "who is this who even sins forgives?" The present tense αφιησιν ("forgives") and the adverb και ("even/also") suggest the dinner guests perceive this as a habitual claim. The question echoes the earlier controversy in Luke 5:21.

40. **7:50 — "Your faith has saved you" (η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε)**: Sinaiticus reads η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε — perfect active indicative of σωζω. The perfect tense σεσωκεν means "has saved you [and you remain saved]." The same formula appears in Luke 8:48, 17:19, and 18:42. Faith, not the act of anointing or weeping, is identified as the instrumental means of salvation.

41. **7:50 — "go in peace" (πορευου εις ειρηνην)**: Sinaiticus reads πορευου εις ειρηνην — "go into peace." The preposition εις ("into") rather than εν ("in") suggests entry into a new state: not merely "go peacefully" but "go into [a state of] peace." This echoes the Hebrew שלום (shalom) — wholeness, well-being, reconciliation with God.
